docs.title: Scenario Based Azure Labs
docs.text:
This is a collection of some scenario based Azure Pipelines using different concepts in Azure.

Service Description Azure Data Factory,Logic App Service Send Email Via Webhook Activity Azure Data Factory,SQL Server,Logic App Service Lookup Activity to fetch user details and send custom Email Azure Data Factory,SQL Server Run a stored procedure from ADF to log pipeline run details into SQL Server table

docs.title: Airflow: Orquestación de Workflows
docs.text: "Airflow es el estándar para automatizar y monitorear pipelines de datos."

docs.title: 🛫 ¿Qué es Apache Airflow?
docs.text: Airflow es una plataforma open source para programar, orquestar y monitorear flujos de trabajo (DAGs) en data engineering. Permite definir dependencias, programar tareas y visualizar la ejecución de pipelines complejos.

docs.title: 🛠️ Componentes Clave
docs.text:
• DAGs: Flujos de trabajo dirigidos y acíclicos.
  • Operadores: Tareas individuales (PythonOperator, BashOperator, etc).
  • Sensors: Espera por eventos o condiciones externas.
  • Hooks: Conexión con bases de datos, APIs y servicios cloud.
  • XComs: Comunicación entre tareas.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Divide y vencerás

Crea DAGs modulares y reutilizables para facilitar el mantenimiento.

Monitorea y alerta

Configura notificaciones y alertas para detectar fallos rápidamente.

Versiona y documenta

Usa control de versiones y docstrings en tus DAGs.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de DAG en Airflow
docs.text:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator
from datetime import datetime

default_args = {
    'owner': 'ericksang',
    'start_date': datetime(2024, 1, 1),
}

dag = DAG('ejemplo_dag', default_args=default_args, schedule_interval='@daily')

tarea1 = BashOperator(
    task_id='inicio',
    bash_command='echo "Inicio del pipeline"',
    dag=dag
)

tarea2 = BashOperator(
    task_id='fin',
    bash_command='echo "Fin del pipeline"',
    dag=dag
)

tarea1 >> tarea2

docs.title: BI & Visualización de Datos
docs.text: "La visualización convierte datos complejos en insights accionables."

docs.title: 📊 ¿Por qué BI y Visualización?
docs.text: Las herramientas de Business Intelligence (BI) y visualización permiten explorar, analizar y comunicar datos de forma efectiva. Son clave para la toma de decisiones y el monitoreo de KPIs en cualquier organización.

docs.title: 🛠️ Herramientas Clave
docs.text:
• Power BI: Dashboards interactivos, integración con múltiples fuentes.
  • Tableau: Visualización avanzada, storytelling de datos.
  • Looker: Modelado de datos y exploración self-service.
  • Grafana: Monitoreo y visualización en tiempo real.
  • Superset: BI open source, dashboards y filtros dinámicos.
  • Metabase: Consultas y visualización sencilla para equipos.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Cuenta una historia con los datos

Usa gráficos claros, títulos descriptivos y resalta los insights clave.

Automatiza la actualización de dashboards

Conecta tus visualizaciones a pipelines y fuentes en tiempo real.

Cuida la experiencia del usuario

Simplifica la navegación y personaliza los dashboards según el público.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de Dashboard en Power BI
docs.text:
• KPIs de ventas, ingresos y clientes
  • Filtros por región y periodo
  • Alertas automáticas por umbrales

docs.title: Cloud & Data Engineering Landscape
docs.text: "El reto no es solo elegir la nube, sino diseñar soluciones escalables, seguras y eficientes para cada etapa del ciclo de datos."

docs.title: 🌐 Visión General: Cloud y Open Source
docs.text: El ecosistema cloud y open source ofrece una variedad de servicios y herramientas para cada etapa del ciclo de vida de datos. El enfoque agnóstico permite comparar y seleccionar la mejor opción según el caso de uso, presupuesto y escalabilidad.

docs.title: 🏗️ Etapas Clave y Soluciones
docs.text: Etapa AWS GCP Azure Open Source / Otros Ingesta Kinesis, Glue, DMS Dataflow, Pub/Sub Event Hubs, Data Factory Kafka, NiFi, Airbyte Almacenamiento S3, Redshift, RDS BigQuery, GCS, CloudSQL Data Lake, Synapse, Blob MinIO, PostgreSQL, Hive Procesamiento EMR, Glue, Lambda Dataproc, Dataflow Databricks, HDInsight Spark, Flink, Dask Orquestación Step Functions, MWAA Composer Data Factory, Logic Apps Airflow, Prefect, Luigi Transformación Glue, Redshift Spectrum Dataflow, BigQuery Synapse, Databricks dbt, Spark, Pandas Streaming Kinesis, MSK Pub/Sub, Dataflow Event Hubs, Stream Analytics Kafka, Pulsar, Flink ML/AI SageMaker, Bedrock Vertex AI, AutoML Azure ML, Cognitive MLflow, TensorFlow, PyTorch Visualización QuickSight Data Studio, Looker Power BI Superset, Metabase, Grafana Seguridad/Gob. IAM, Lake Formation IAM, DLP, Security Command Center Azure AD, Purview Vault, Ranger, Open Policy Agent

docs.title: 🔍 Problemáticas Comunes
docs.text:
• Integración de fuentes heterogéneas
  • Escalabilidad y performance
  • Costos y optimización
  • Seguridad y gobernanza
  • Latencia y procesamiento en tiempo real
  • Portabilidad entre nubes y on-premise

docs.title: 💡 Soluciones y Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Arquitectura modular y desacoplada

Diseña sistemas con componentes independientes para facilitar el cambio de tecnología y la escalabilidad.

Automatización y monitoreo

Usa orquestadores y herramientas de observabilidad para detectar cuellos de botella y anticipar problemas.

Open Source como habilitador

Herramientas como Airflow, dbt, Spark y Kafka permiten flexibilidad, comunidad y reducción de costos.

docs.title: Data Engineering
docs.text: "Transformando datos en valor real para el negocio."

docs.title: 🚀 ¿Qué es Data Engineering?
docs.text: La ingeniería de datos es el arte de diseñar, construir y mantener sistemas que permiten la recolección, almacenamiento, procesamiento y análisis eficiente de grandes volúmenes de datos. Es la base para la analítica avanzada, la inteligencia artificial y la toma de decisiones basada en datos.

docs.title: 🛠️ Herramientas y Tecnologías Clave
docs.text:
• ETL/ELT: Airflow, dbt, Spark, Talend, Dataflow
  • Orquestación: Apache Airflow, Prefect
  • Procesamiento: Apache Spark, Databricks, Flink
  • Almacenamiento: Data Lakes (S3, GCS), Data Warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift)
  • Streaming: Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis
  • Automatización: Python, Bash, Terraform

docs.title: 🧩 Arquitectura Moderna
docs.text:
graph TD;
  FUENTE[Fuentes de Datos] --> INGESTA[Ingesta]
  INGESTA --> PROCESAMIENTO[Procesamiento]
  PROCESAMIENTO --> ALMACENAMIENTO[Almacenamiento]
  ALMACENAMIENTO --> ANALITICA[Analítica]
  ANALITICA --> VISUALIZACION[Visualización]

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Automatiza todo lo que puedas

Usa orquestadores y scripts para reducir errores manuales y mejorar la escalabilidad.

Documenta y versiona tus pipelines

dbt y Airflow permiten mantener control y trazabilidad sobre los flujos de datos.

Monitorea y alerta

Implementa monitoreo y alertas para detectar fallos y cuellos de botella a tiempo.

docs.title: 📚 Recursos Recomendados
docs.text:
• The Data Engineering Cookbook
  • Awesome Data Engineering
  • Airflow Best Practices
  • dbt Documentation

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de Pipeline ETL con Airflow
docs.text:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

def extract():
    # Extracción de datos
    pass

def transform():
    # Transformación de datos
    pass

def load():
    # Carga de datos
    pass

default_args = {
    'owner': 'ericksang',
    'start_date': datetime(2023, 1, 1),
}

dag = DAG('etl_pipeline', default_args=default_args, schedule_interval='@daily')

extract_task = PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=extract, dag=dag)
transform_task = PythonOperator(task_id='transform', python_callable=transform, dag=dag)
load_task = PythonOperator(task_id='load', python_callable=load, dag=dag)

extract_task >> transform_task >> load_task

¿Tienes dudas o quieres ver ejemplos de otros flujos? ¡Contáctame o revisa los notebooks embebidos!

docs.title: Databricks: Plataforma Unificada de Datos
docs.text: "Databricks acelera el ciclo de vida de datos y machine learning en la nube."

docs.title: 🔥 ¿Qué es Databricks?
docs.text: Databricks es una plataforma cloud que integra Apache Spark, Delta Lake y MLflow para procesamiento de datos, análisis avanzado y machine learning colaborativo. Permite trabajar con notebooks, pipelines y dashboards en un entorno seguro y escalable.

docs.title: 🛠️ Componentes Clave
docs.text:
• Notebooks colaborativos: Python, SQL, Scala, R
  • Clusters escalables: Spark, autoscaling, integración cloud
  • Delta Lake: Almacenamiento transaccional y versionado
  • MLflow: Gestión de experimentos y modelos ML
  • Jobs: Automatización y orquestación de pipelines
  • Dashboards: Visualización interactiva

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Versiona y documenta tus notebooks

Usa comentarios, markdown y control de versiones para reproducibilidad.

Optimiza recursos

Configura autoscaling y monitorea el uso de clusters.

Integra con el ecosistema cloud

Conecta Databricks con S3, ADLS, BigQuery y otras fuentes.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de Notebook en Databricks
docs.text:
# Databricks notebook source
import pyspark.sql.functions as F
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate()
df = spark.read.csv('/databricks-datasets/airlines/part-00000', header=True)
df = df.withColumn('Year', F.col('Year').cast('int'))
df.groupBy('Year').count().show()

docs.title: dbt: Modelado y Transformación de Datos
docs.text: "dbt permite transformar, documentar y testear datos con SQL de forma colaborativa."

docs.title: 🧬 ¿Qué es dbt?
docs.text: dbt (data build tool) es una herramienta open source para modelar, transformar y documentar datos en el data warehouse usando SQL. Facilita la colaboración, el versionado y el testing de modelos de datos.

docs.title: 🛠️ Componentes Clave
docs.text:
• Models: Scripts SQL que definen transformaciones.
  • Seeds: Datos estáticos para pruebas y referencia.
  • Snapshots: Versionado histórico de datos.
  • Tests: Validaciones automáticas de calidad y consistencia.
  • Docs: Documentación interactiva de modelos y relaciones.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Versiona y documenta cada modelo

Usa descripciones, docstrings y control de versiones en tus scripts.

Automatiza el testing

Implementa tests de unicidad, nulos y relaciones para asegurar calidad.

Integra con CI/CD

Ejecuta dbt en pipelines automáticos para validar y desplegar cambios.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de Modelo y Test en dbt
docs.text:
-- models/ventas_diarias.sql
SELECT fecha, SUM(monto) AS total
FROM ventas
GROUP BY fecha;

# tests/ventas_diarias.yml
tests:
  - unique:
      column_name: fecha
  - not_null:
      column_name: total

docs.title: Kafka: Streaming de Datos en Tiempo Real
docs.text: "Kafka es el estándar para integrar y procesar flujos de datos en tiempo real."

docs.title: ⚡ ¿Qué es Apache Kafka?
docs.text: Apache Kafka es una plataforma distribuida para publicar, suscribir, almacenar y procesar flujos de datos en tiempo real. Es fundamental en arquitecturas modernas de data engineering, IoT y microservicios.

docs.title: 🛠️ Componentes Clave
docs.text:
• Topics: Canales de comunicación para los mensajes.
  • Producers: Publican datos en los topics.
  • Consumers: Procesan y leen los datos de los topics.
  • Brokers: Servidores que gestionan la distribución y almacenamiento.
  • ZooKeeper: Coordina y gestiona el clúster.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Diseña topics por dominio de negocio

Facilita la escalabilidad y el mantenimiento.

Monitorea el lag y la latencia

Usa herramientas como Kafka Manager, Grafana y Prometheus.

Asegura la durabilidad y el orden

Configura la replicación y las políticas de retención adecuadas.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de Productor y Consumidor en Python
docs.text:
from kafka import KafkaProducer, KafkaConsumer

producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers='localhost:9092')
producer.send('mi_topic', b'Hola Kafka!')
producer.flush()

consumer = KafkaConsumer('mi_topic', bootstrap_servers='localhost:9092')
for msg in consumer:
    print(msg.value)

docs.title: Notebooks: Ejemplos y Tutoriales
docs.text: "Los notebooks son el laboratorio interactivo del data engineer."

docs.title: 📓 ¿Por qué usar Notebooks?
docs.text: Los notebooks (Jupyter, Databricks, Zeppelin) permiten combinar código, visualizaciones y documentación en un solo entorno interactivo. Son ideales para prototipar, compartir y reproducir experimentos de datos.

docs.title: 🛠️ Tipos de Notebooks
docs.text:
• Jupyter: Python, R, Julia, integración con librerías de datos y visualización.
  • Databricks: Spark, Delta Lake, MLflow, colaboración cloud.
  • Zeppelin: Multi-lenguaje, integración con Hadoop y Spark.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Documenta cada paso

Usa celdas markdown para explicar el objetivo y los resultados.

Versiona y comparte

Guarda los notebooks en repositorios y publícalos en plataformas colaborativas.

Integra con pipelines

Usa notebooks como parte de flujos ETL y experimentos de ML.

docs.title: Others
docs.text: Otros temas y herramientas relevantes para ingeniería de datos.

docs.title: Big Data & Landscape General
docs.text: "Big Data no es solo volumen, es velocidad, variedad y valor."

docs.title: 🌎 ¿Qué es Big Data?
docs.text: Big Data se refiere al manejo y procesamiento de grandes volúmenes de datos que no pueden ser gestionados con herramientas tradicionales. Implica trabajar con datos estructurados y no estructurados, provenientes de múltiples fuentes y en tiempo real.

docs.title: 🏞️ Landscape General de Data Engineering
docs.text:
graph TD;
  FUENTES[Fuentes de Datos] --> INGESTA[Ingesta]
  INGESTA --> PROCESAMIENTO[Procesamiento]
  PROCESAMIENTO --> ALMACENAMIENTO[Almacenamiento]
  ALMACENAMIENTO --> ANALITICA[Analítica]
  ANALITICA --> ML[Machine Learning]
  ANALITICA --> VISUALIZACION[Visualización]
  ML --> PRODUCTO[Producto/Servicio]

docs.title: 🏢 Ecosistema de Big Data
docs.text:
• Procesamiento distribuido: Apache Spark, Hadoop, Flink
  • Almacenamiento escalable: HDFS, S3, BigQuery, Snowflake
  • Streaming: Kafka, Kinesis, Pulsar
  • Orquestación: Airflow, Luigi, Prefect
  • Machine Learning: MLlib, TensorFlow, PyTorch
  • Visualización: Power BI, Tableau, Grafana

docs.title: 💡 Retos y Oportunidades
docs.text:
Retos

  • Escalabilidad y performance
  • Seguridad y gobernanza
  • Integración de fuentes heterogéneas

Oportunidades

  • Analítica avanzada
  • Personalización de productos
  • Automatización inteligente

docs.text:

  • Spark Documentation
  • Hadoop Ecosystem

docs.title: Otros Temas y Herramientas
docs.text: "El ecosistema data engineering evoluciona constantemente."

docs.title: 🧰 Herramientas y Temas Relevantes
docs.text:
• Airbyte: Ingesta de datos open source, alternativa a Fivetran y Talend.
  • Prefect: Orquestación moderna, fácil integración con cloud y Python.
  • Luigi: Orquestador de workflows por Spotify, robusto y flexible.
  • Terraform: Infraestructura como código para despliegue cloud.
  • Great Expectations: Validación y calidad de datos automatizada.
  • Vault: Gestión de secretos y credenciales.
  • Open Policy Agent: Gobernanza y control de acceso.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Explora y experimenta

Prueba nuevas herramientas y comparte tus hallazgos con la comunidad.

Automatiza la infraestructura

Usa IaC (Infrastructure as Code) para reproducibilidad y escalabilidad.

Contribuye a open source

Participa en proyectos, reporta bugs y comparte mejoras.

¿Quieres saber más sobre arquitecturas Big Data o ver ejemplos prácticos? ¡Explora los notebooks y recursos del sitio!

docs.title: Python para Data Engineering
docs.text: "Python es el motor de la automatización y el procesamiento de datos moderno."

docs.title: 🐍 ¿Por qué Python?
docs.text: Python es el lenguaje más popular en data engineering por su simplicidad, versatilidad y enorme ecosistema de librerías. Permite construir pipelines, automatizar tareas, procesar datos y conectar con servicios cloud y open source.

docs.title: 🔧 Librerías Clave
docs.text:
• Pandas: Manipulación y análisis de datos tabulares.
  • PySpark: Procesamiento distribuido sobre Apache Spark.
  • SQLAlchemy: Conexión y modelado de bases de datos.
  • Requests: Integración con APIs y servicios externos.
  • Airflow: Orquestación de workflows.
  • dbt: Transformación y modelado de datos.
  • Dask: Procesamiento paralelo y escalable.
  • Great Expectations: Validación y calidad de datos.

docs.title: 🛠️ Ejemplo de Pipeline en Python
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
import requests

def extract():
    response = requests.get('https://api.example.com/data')
    return pd.DataFrame(response.json())

def transform(df):
    df = df.dropna()
    df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
    return df

def load(df):
    df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False)

def main():
    data = extract()
    clean_data = transform(data)
    load(clean_data)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Escribe código modular y testeable

Divide tus scripts en funciones y módulos reutilizables.

Documenta y versiona

Usa docstrings, comentarios y control de versiones (Git).

Automatiza y monitorea

Integra tus scripts con Airflow, Prefect o cron para ejecución automática y monitoreo.

docs.title: Spark: Procesamiento Distribuido de Datos
docs.text: "Spark permite procesar grandes volúmenes de datos de forma rápida y escalable."

docs.title: ⚡ ¿Qué es Apache Spark?
docs.text: Apache Spark es un motor open source para procesamiento distribuido de datos, ideal para ETL, machine learning y análisis avanzado en grandes volúmenes de información.

docs.title: 🛠️ Componentes Clave
docs.text:
• Spark SQL: Consultas y transformaciones con SQL.
  • DataFrames: Estructuras tabulares para manipulación eficiente.
  • Spark Streaming: Procesamiento de datos en tiempo real.
  • MLlib: Machine learning distribuido.
  • GraphX: Análisis de grafos.
  • PySpark: API en Python para Spark.

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Optimiza el uso de memoria

Usa particiones, persistencia y evita acciones innecesarias.

Divide el procesamiento en etapas

Encadena transformaciones y acciones para mayor eficiencia.

Monitorea y ajusta recursos

Configura el cluster y revisa el Spark UI para identificar cuellos de botella.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de ETL con PySpark
docs.text:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate()
df = spark.read.csv('data.csv', header=True)
df = df.dropna()
df.groupBy('categoria').count().show()

docs.title: SQL en Data Engineering
docs.text: "SQL es el lenguaje universal para consultar, transformar y modelar datos."

docs.title: 🗄️ ¿Por qué SQL?
docs.text: SQL (Structured Query Language) es esencial en data engineering para interactuar con bases de datos relacionales y cloud, modelar datos, optimizar consultas y construir reportes.

docs.title: 🛠️ Motores y Herramientas
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL: Base de datos relacional open source, robusta y escalable.
  • BigQuery: Data warehouse serverless de Google Cloud.
  • Snowflake: Plataforma cloud para almacenamiento y análisis de datos.
  • Redshift: Data warehouse de AWS.
  • SQL Server: Solución empresarial de Microsoft.
  • SQLite: Base de datos ligera y embebida.
  • dbt: Modelado y transformación de datos con SQL.

docs.title: 🧩 Modelado y Transformación
docs.text:
• Normalización y desnormalización
  • Joins, subconsultas y CTEs
  • Funciones de ventana y agregación
  • Optimización de índices y particiones
  • Testing y documentación con dbt

docs.title: 💡 Buenas Prácticas
docs.text:
Escribe consultas legibles y eficientes

Usa CTEs, comentarios y evita subconsultas innecesarias.

Versiona y documenta tus modelos

dbt permite mantener control y trazabilidad sobre los modelos SQL.

Optimiza el performance

Analiza planes de ejecución, usa índices y particiones.

docs.title: 📝 Ejemplo de Consulta Avanzada
docs.text:
WITH ventas_diarias AS (
  SELECT fecha, SUM(monto) AS total
  FROM ventas
  WHERE fecha >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY fecha
)
SELECT fecha, total
FROM ventas_diarias
WHERE total > 1000
ORDER BY fecha DESC;

  • Produce & Consume Message in JSON format
  • Register the Schema in the Schema Registry
  • Use JSONSerializer & JSONDeserializer - Fetch schema from Schema Registry to Validate & Serialize /- Deserialize (Redpanda Schema Registry Does not Support JSON)
  • Produce Message in AVRO format
  • Consume Message in AVRO format
  • Send Key and Headers (example- Coorelation_ID) with every message
  • On_delivery callback vs flush()
  • Producer Configs (compression.type & message.max.bytes)
  • Producer Configs (batch.size & linger.ms)
  • Log compaction
  • Deleting Records in Kafka aka Kafka Tombstone
  • auto_offset_reset (Consumer Configuration)
  • Consumer Group
  • Schema Evolution & Schema Compatibility
  • Special topic called _consumer_offsets & _schemas topic
  • Kcat (Kafka cat commands)

docs.title: Index
docs.text: Bunch of code notebooks

docs.title: Computing Engine:
docs.text:
Spark is not storage platform we can store the data in hdfs, rdbms etc...

Spark can process terabytes of data in distributed manner.

docs.title: Compute Cluster:
docs.text:
• each slave has 16 GB RAM, 1TB storage and 4 core CPU
  • even master has some data and RAM
  • the above cluster can compute 64 gb of data at a time.
  • the master divides the data among the slave nodes and then slaves process the data.

docs.title: Lecture 2 : Why Apache Spark?
docs.text:
Different Databases

new formats like video, audio, json,avro started coming in but rdms cannot handle it.

volume of data also increased.

docs.title: What is Big Data?
docs.text: Data Lake works on Extract Load Transform architecture

docs.title: Issues with RDBMS
docs.text:
• Storage
  • Processing - RAM and CPU

Enter Spark...

docs.title: Misconception:
docs.text:
• Hadoop is a database - its not a database just a filesystem (hdfs)
  • Spark is 100 times faster than hadoop
  • Spark processes data in RAM but Hadoop doesnt

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
Hadoop does lot of read write IO operations and sends data back and forth to the disk.

But in spark each executor has its own memory.

Where is there no difference?

When we have very less data like 10 GB, there is no difference because the hadoop cluster also doesnt write to the disk it fits first time in memory.

docs.title: Ease of Use
docs.text: Spark has both low level and high level API in Python which is easier than using Hive. Low level programming is on RDD level.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Hadoop has in built Kerberos Authentication via YARN whereas Spark doesnt have any security mechanism.

  • The authentication helps create ACL lists at directory level in HDFS.

  • Spark uses HDFS Storage so it gets ACL feature / ability and when it uses YARN it gets Kerberos Authentication.

docs.title: Fault Tolerance
docs.text:
Data Replication in Hadoop

HDFS keeps track of which node / rack has the data from A B C and D

DAG in Spark

  • So Spark computes / transforms in multiple processes Process 1 -> Process 2 -> Process 3 ....
  • After each process the data is stored in a data structure called RDD which is immutable. So even if there is a failure Spark engine knows how to reconstruct the data for a particular process from the RDD at that stage.

docs.title: Lecture 4 : Spark Ecosystem
docs.text:
High Level API : We cna write any SQL queries in python,java etc... there are ML and GraphX librries also.

We can write code in many languages. Low Level API : we can make RDD's and work on them.

docs.title: Where does Spark run?
docs.text:
Spark Engine would need some memory for transformation.

  • suppose it needs 4 worker nodes each 20 GB and a driver node of 20 gb.
  • it goes to the cluster manager and asks for total 100 GB of memory, if available then the manager will assign that muuch storage.
  • cluster manager is also called YARN, K8S, Standalone managers

docs.title: Lecture 5 : Read Modes in Spark
docs.text: format -> data file format : csv,json,jdbc and odbc connection. Format is optional parameter, by default its parquet format option -> inferschema, mode and header [optional field] schema -> manual schema can be passed here load -> path from where we need to read the data [not optional]

docs.title: DataframeReader API
docs.text: Access it using 'spark.read' in the spark session

docs.title: Spark Cluster
docs.text:
• 20 core per machine and 100 GB RAM / each machine
  • Total Cluster : 200 cores and 1TB RAM
  • The master is controlled by Resource Manager and the workers are controlled by Node Manager.

docs.title: What happens when user submits code?
docs.text:
• The user submits some Spark code for execution to the Resource Manager. It needs 20 GB RAM, 25 GB executor, 5 total executors and 5 CPU cores.
  • So the manager goes to W5 and asks to create 20GB container as the driver node.

docs.title: Driver Allocation
docs.text:
Now this 20 GB driver is called Application Master

There are two main() functions inside the master, one is for PySpark and other is for JVM like Java,Scala etc...

The JVM main() is called Application Driver.

The Spark Core has a Java Wrapper and the Java Wrapper has a Python Wrapper.

When we write code in PySpark it gets converted to Java Wrapper.

The PySpark driver is not a requirement but the Java Wrapper is required to run any code.

docs.title: Worker Allocation
docs.text: • Now the Application master asks for the executors to be assigned and the resource manager allocates.

docs.title: Executor Container
docs.text:
Each executor has 5 core CPU and 25GB RAM.

THe above is when we have pure Java code and dont use Python UDF.

But what if we use Python UDF functions?

We need a Python worker inside the executor to be able to run the code.

docs.title: StructType and StructField
docs.text:
Example:

How to skip the header row?

df = spark.read.option("skipRows", 2).csv("file.csv")

docs.title: Lecture 8 : Handling Corrupter Records in Spark
How many records in each mode?
How to Print Corrupted Records
docs.text: Output

docs.title: How to Store Corrupted Records
docs.text: The corrupted records are in json format

docs.title: Lecture 9 : Transformations and Actions in Spark
Types of Transformations
docs.text:
• Narrow Transformation
  • Wide Transformation

Example:

Suppose data is of 200MB. 200MB / 128MB = 2 partitions

Let's say both partitions go to separate executors.

Q1 : Filtering Records There is no data movement here.

Q2: Find Total Income of each employee

One id = 2 record is in one partition and the other is in the second partition so we need to do wide transformation

Data needs to be shuffled and records with same id must be moved to same partition.

  • filter,select,union etc are narrow transformations
  • join,groupby,distinct

docs.title: Lecture 10 : DAG and Lazy Evaluation in Spark
docs.text:
• For every action there is a new job, here there are three actions : read,inferSchema,sum and show
  • When used with groupBy().sum(): It is considered an action because it triggers computation to aggregate data across partitions and produce a result. This operation forces Spark to execute the transformations leading up to it, effectively creating a job.

  • When used as a column expression df.select(sum("value")): It acts more like a transformation in Spark's context, especially if part of a larger query or pipeline that does not immediately trigger execution. In this case, it only defines the operation and does not create a job until an action (like show() or collect()) is called.

  • Job for reading file Whole Stage Codegen - generate Java ByteCode

  • Inferschema

  • GroupBy and Count As explained above this is an action.

  • Show Final action to display df

After we read the csv and inferSchema there are no jobs created since filter and repartition both are transformations not actions.

When there are two filters on same dataset

This is the job

docs.title: Optimizations on the Filter
docs.text: Both the filters are on the same task The optimizations can be applied because Spark is lazily evaluated.

docs.title: Lecture 11: Working with JSON Data in Spark
docs.text:
Two types of JSON notation:

  • Line Delimited JSON

  • Multi Line JSON 

[
{
  "name": "Manish",
  "age": 20,
  "salary": 20000
},
{
  "name": "Nikita",
  "age": 25,
  "salary": 21000
},
{
  "name": "Pritam",
  "age": 16,
  "salary": 22000
},
{
  "name": "Prantosh",
  "age": 35,
  "salary": 25000
},
{
  "name": "Vikash",
  "age": 67,
  "salary": 40000
}
]

Line Delimited JSON is more efficient in terms of performance because the compiler knows that each line has one JSON record whereas in multiline json the compiler needs to keept track of where the record ends and the next one starts.

docs.title: Different number of keys in each line
docs.text: Here what happens is that the line with the extra key has the value while for the rest its null

docs.title: Multiline Incorrect JSON
docs.text:
We dont pass a list here rather its just dictionaries 

{
  "name": "Manish",
  "age": 20,
  "salary": 20000
},
{
  "name": "Nikita",
  "age": 25,
  "salary": 21000
},
{
  "name": "Pritam",
  "age": 16,
  "salary": 22000
},
{
  "name": "Prantosh",
  "age": 35,
  "salary": 25000
},
{
  "name": "Vikash",
  "age": 67,
  "salary": 40000
}
When we process the json it just reads the first dictionary as a record and the rest is not processed.

docs.title: Corrupted Records
docs.text: We dont need to define _corrupted_record in the schema, it will add the column on its ownn

docs.title: How is Spark Code compiled?
docs.text: • The catalyst optimizer creates a plan and creates RDD lineage

docs.title: Workflow Diagram
docs.text:
• Unresolved Logical Plan : Bunch of crude steps to execute the SQL code
  • Catalog : The table, files and database metadata information si stored in the catalog. Suppose we call read.csv on file that doesnt exist. The procedure that gives / throws the error is assisted via the catalog. In Analysis phase, we go through these steps. If some file/table is not found then we get Analysis Exception This error occurs when the Logical plan provided is not able to be resolved.
  • Reoslved Logical Plan : This is the phase when we finished analysing the catalog objects.
  • Logical Optimization: There are many examples. Suppose we need just two columns in select output, the spark engine does not fetch all the columns rather jsut fetches the two columns from memory that we need. Another example is when we use multiple filters on the same column in different lines of code. When we execute this code, we see that all of it is executed with or statements in one single line of code.
  • Physical Plan: This involves taking decision like the type of join to use: Broadcast Join is one example. From the logical plan, we can build multiple physical plans. Thebest Physical Plan is a set of RDDs to be run on different executors on the cluster.

docs.title: Data Storage in RDD
docs.text:
Suppose we have 500MB of data and 128MB partition, so we will have 4 partitions.

The data is scattered on various executors.

Its not in single contiguous location like elements of a list. The data structure used ot process this data is called RDD

Why is RDD recoverable?

  • RDD is immutable. If we apply multiple filters each dataset after filtering is a different dataset

  • In below case if rdd2 fails then we can restore rdd1 because of the lineage.

docs.title: Disadvantage of RDD
docs.text: • No optimization done by Spark on RDD. The dev must specify explicitly on how to optimize RDD.

docs.title: Advantage
docs.text:
• Works well with unstructured data where there are no columns and rows / key-value pairs
  • RDD is type safe, we get error on compile time rather than runtime which happens with Dataframe API.

docs.title: Avoiding RDDs
docs.text:
• RDD : How to do? Dataframe API: Just specify what to do?

You can see in above case that we have a join and filter but we are specifically saying that first join then filter so it triggers a shuffle first and then filter which is not beneficial.

docs.title: Lecture 14 : Parquet File Internals
docs.text:
There are two types of file formats:

  • Columnar Based and Row Based

docs.title: Physical Storage of Data on Disk
Write Once Read Many
docs.text:
The funda of big data is write once read many.

  • We dont need all the columns for analytics of big data, so columnar storage is the best.
  • If we store in row based format then we need to jump many memory racks to be able to get the data we need.

  • OLTP generally use row base4d file format.
  • I/O should be reduced so OLAP uses columnar format.

docs.title: Why Columnar format may not be the best?
docs.text:
In above case we can get col1 and col2 easily but for col10 we still need to scan the entire file.

To tackle this:

Let's say we have 100 million total rows.

We can store 100,000 records at a time, continuously in one row, then the next 100,000 records in next row and so on in hybrid format.

docs.title: Logical Partitioning in Parquet
docs.text:
Let's day we have 500mb data, each row group by default has 128 mb data, so we will have 4 row groups. Each row group will have some metadata attached to it.

In our example let's say one row group has 100000 records. The column is futher stored as a page.

docs.title: Runlength Encoding and Bitpacking
docs.text:
Suppose we have 10 lakh records but there can be say 4 countries.

So parquet actually creates a dictionary of key value pair with key as int starting from 0 to 3 and then in the dictionary encoded data, we can see the keys being used insted of country name.

docs.title: Demo
docs.text:
parquet-tools inspect <filename>

Gives some file and brief column level metadata.

parquet_file.metadata.row_group(0).column_group(0)

Compression is GZIP

Encoding is explained on top.

docs.title: Bitpacking Advantage
docs.text:
• Bitpacking helps in compressing the bits so in above case we just have 4 unique values and hence we need just 2 bytes.
  • Query in seconds for running select on csv,parquet etc..

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Here the actual data is stored in the pages and it has metadata like min,max and count.

  • Let's say we need to find out people less than 18 years age

Here when we divide data into row groups, we dont need to do any IO read operation on Row group 2, it saves lot of time and optimize performance.

The above concept is called Predicate Pushdown.

docs.title: Projection Pruning
docs.text: Projection Pruning means we dont read IO from columns that are not part of the select query or that arent required for any join.

docs.title: Lecture 15 : How to write data on the disk?
Modes to write data
docs.text:
Create three files 

  write_df = read_df.repartition(3).write.format("csv")\
    .option("header", "True")\
    .mode("overwrite")\  # Using .mode() instead of .option() for overwrite mode
    .option("path", "/FileStore/tables/Write_Data/")\
    .save()

docs.title: Lecture 16: Partitioning and Bucketing
docs.text:
In above data we cannot partition by any column because there is no similarity but we can bucket the data.

In above data we can partition by the country, but again we might have more data in India partition and less data in Japan.

docs.title: Example of Partitioning
docs.text:
The advantage is that the entire data is not scanned and only few partitions are scanned based on the filters.

Partitioning by Id

Here we can see that we have created partitions by ID and since ID is low cardinality column partitioning is not efficient and we need to use bucketing.

docs.title: Bucketing by Id
docs.text: Dividing into 3 buckets

docs.title: Tasks vs Buckets
docs.text: • If we have 200 partitions we will have 200 tasks and each task will create 5 buckets each, to tackle this we first repartition into 5 partitions and then bucketBy 5.

docs.title: How does bucketing help with joins?
docs.text: • Here we can see that since we have same column bucket on both tables the ids can be easily mapped and there is no shuffling.

docs.title: Bucket Pruning
docs.text: Suppose we have 1M Aadhar Ids and we divide into 100,000 each bucket so when we divide the above aadhar id by 100000 then we get the exact bucket where the number lies in.

docs.title: Lecture 17 : Spark Session vs Spark Context
docs.text:
• Spark Session is entry point to the Spark cluster where we provide the parameters to create and operate our cluster.
  • Spark session will have different context like one for SQL, PySpark etc...

docs.title: Lecture 18: Job, Stage and Tasks
docs.text:
• One Application is created.
  • One job is created per action.
  • One stage is defined for every transformation like filter.
  • Task is the actually activity on the data that's happening.

docs.title: Complete flow diagram
docs.text:
Every job has minimum one stage and one task.

Repartition to filter is one job because we dont hit an action in between.

Every wide dependency transformation has its own stage. All narrow dependency transformations come in one stage as a DAG.

docs.title: How do tasks get created? [Read and Write Exchange]
docs.text:
• The repartition stage actually is a wide dependency transformation and creates two partitions from one, its a Write exchange of data.
  • Now the filter and select stage reads this repartitioned data(Read exchange) and filter creates two tasks because we have two partitions.
  • Next we need to find out how many folks earn > 90000 and age > 25 so we need to do a groupby that's a wide dependency transformation and it creates another stage. By default there are 200 partitions created.
  • So some partitions may have data and some wont.

docs.title: Lecture 17: Dataframe Transformations in Spark Part 1
docs.text:
Data gets stored in Row() format in the form of bytes

Columns are expressions. Expressions are set of transformations on more than one value in a record.

docs.title: Ways to select values / columns
docs.text:
Column Manipulations

Other methods

selectExpr

Aliasing Columns

docs.title: Lecutre 18 : Dataframe Transformations in Spark Part II
Literals in spark
docs.text: Used to pass same value in all the columns

docs.title: Adding Columns
docs.text: If the column already exists then it gets overwritten.

docs.title: Lecture 19: union vs unionAll()
docs.text:
We can see that here we have a duplicate id

In PySpark union and unionAll behaves in the same way, both retain duplicates

But in Spark SQL when we do union it drops the duplicate records

docs.title: Selecting data and unioning the same table
What happens when we change the order of the columns?
docs.text:
wrong_manager_df actually has the wrong order of columns but still we get the union output but in a wrong column values.

If we give different number of columns an exception is thrown.

If we use unionByName then the column names on both dfs must be the same.

docs.title: Lecture 19: Repartitioning and Coalesce
docs.text: Suppose we have 5 partitions and one of them is skewed a lot 100MB, let's say this is the best selling product records. This partition takes lot of time to compute. So the other executors have to wait until this executor finishes processing.

docs.title: Repartitioning
docs.text: Suppose we have the above partitions and total data is 100mb. let's say we do repartition(5) so we will have 5 partitions now for the data with 40mb per partition.

docs.title: Coalesce
docs.text:
In case of coalesce there is no equal splitting of partition memory, rather the already existing partitions get merged together.

There is no shuffling in coalesce but in repartitioning there is shuffling of data.

docs.title: Pros and Cons in repartitioning
docs.text:
• There is evenly distributed data.
  • Con is that IO operations are more, its expensive.
  • Con of coalesce is that the data is unevenly distributed.

Repartitioning can increase or decrease the partitions but coalescing can only decrease the partitions.

docs.title: How to get number of partitions?
docs.text:
flight_df.rdd.getNumPartitions() gets the initial number of partitions and then we can repartition flight_df.repartition(4). Data is evenly distributed.

Repartitioning based on columns

Since we asked for 300 partitions and we have 255 records some partitions will have null record.

docs.title: Coalescing
docs.text:
Suppose we have 8 partitions and we coalesce into 3 partitions. Coalesce has only one arg.

Uneven distribution of data in partitions.

Apply logic on one column then process if else logic
distinct()
docs.text:
Original Data

Distinct Data

⚠️ Distinct takes no arguments we need to select the columns first and then apply distinct.

docs.title: Dropping duplicate records
docs.text: Point to note is that the dataframe manager_df has no changes, it just shows the records after dups have been dropped.

docs.title: sort()
docs.text:
Descending order

Sorting on multiple columns

Here first the salary is srranged in desc order then we arrange the name in asc order from those records with same salary.

docs.title: Count as both Action and Transformation
docs.text:
⚠️ When we are doing count on a single column and there is a null in it, its not considered in the count. But for all columns we have nulls in the count.

Job created in first case and its not created in second case below.

docs.title: Lecture 23: Group By In Spark
docs.text: Sample Data

docs.title: Questions
docs.text: Salary per department using groupBy()

docs.title: Where do we use window functions?
docs.text:
Suppose we need to find out the percentage of total salary from a particular dept that the person is earning. we can use window function to specify the total salary per department in the particular record itself like I've shown below.

This way we dont need to perform a join.
